data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) After Its Strong Multi‑Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are asking whether Enterprise Products Partners is still reasonably priced after a long run in the unit price, this article walks through what the current valuation really reflects and where the key pressure points are.
  • The units last closed at US$38.03, with stated returns of 0.1% over 7 days, 1.6% over 30 days, 18.3% year to date, 33.3% over 1 year, 83.1% over 3 years and 134.3% over 5 years, which gives plenty of context for a closer look at value.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Enterprise Products Partners in the context of broader interest in energy infrastructure and income focused investments, which has kept attention on its distribution profile and asset base. This backdrop helps explain why valuation has become a key talking point as investors weigh income, risk and price.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Enterprise Products Partners scores 5 out of 6, so the next sections compare what different valuation methods suggest about the current price and then finish with a way to tie those numbers to the broader investment story.

Find out why Enterprise Products Partners's 33.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Enterprise Products Partners Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Enterprise Products Partners, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach built on cash flow projections. The latest twelve months Free Cash Flow is about $4.18b, and analysts plus extrapolations point to projected Free Cash Flow of about $8.14b in 2030, with a path of forecast and estimated cash flows in between. Simply Wall St uses analyst estimates where available, then extends the trend for the outer years.

On this basis, the DCF model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about $92.83 per unit, compared with the recent unit price of $38.03. That gap implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 59.0%, indicating that, based purely on these cash flow assumptions, the units appear materially undervalued on this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Enterprise Products Partners is undervalued by 59.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 51 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Enterprise Products Partners.

Approach 2: Enterprise Products Partners Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business that is regularly generating earnings, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it connects what you pay per unit today to the earnings that are already in place.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Enterprise Products Partners currently trades on a P/E of 14.1x. That sits close to the Oil and Gas industry average of about 14.6x, and below the broader peer group average of 21.4x. This gives a rough sense of how the market is pricing its earnings relative to others.

Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” goes further and estimates what P/E might be reasonable given Enterprise Products Partners earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors. Because it adjusts for these company specific features, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with industry and peer averages.

For Enterprise Products Partners, the Fair Ratio stands at 27.7x, which is higher than the current 14.1x. On this earnings based yardstick, the units screen as undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives on Simply Wall St let you spell out your story for Enterprise Products Partners in plain language, translate that story into assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins, and then connect those assumptions to a fair value that can be compared directly with today’s price. data.title: Does Rubrik (RBRK) Offer Value After Recent Share Price Rebound?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Rubrik's current share price reflects its true worth, this breakdown will help you connect the recent moves in the stock to what the underlying valuation signals are saying.
  • Rubrik last closed at US$55.07, with returns of 5.3% over the past week and 13.3% over the past month, while the 1 year return sits at a 24.9% decline.
  • Recent trading interest has been influenced by ongoing attention on data security and cloud software providers, as investors reassess how these types of businesses are priced in the market. This context helps frame Rubrik's pullback in year to date and 1 year returns against more recent positive momentum.
  • Rubrik currently holds a value score of 2 out of 6. This means it screens as undervalued on only a minority of the checks that will be examined next. The following sections will walk through those valuation methods, with a broader way to think about Rubrik's value story saved for the end of the article.

Rubrik scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Rubrik Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a stock could be worth by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required return. It focuses on what the business may generate in cash for shareholders over time, rather than just current earnings multiples.

For Rubrik, the DCF uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, starting from last twelve months free cash flow of about $240.3 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for several years, and Simply Wall St then extends those cash flow projections further out. Under this framework, projected free cash flow for 2031 is $904.2 million, with additional estimates continuing out to 2035, all expressed in dollars and discounted back to today.

Bringing these discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of $79.89 per share. Versus the recent share price of $55.07, the model implies Rubrik trades at roughly a 31.1% discount to this DCF estimate, which screens as undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Rubrik is undervalued by 31.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 51 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Rubrik.

Approach 2: Rubrik Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are not yet a steady guide, the P/S ratio is often more useful than P/E or P/B, because it relates the share price to revenue, which tends to be more stable while profits are still being built out.

What counts as a reasonable P/S multiple depends on factors such as expected growth, profitability and risk. Higher growth and stronger margins can support higher P/S levels, while higher risk usually calls for a lower multiple.

Rubrik currently trades on a P/S ratio of 8.61x. This sits above the wider Software industry average P/S of 3.77x and above a peer group average of 4.86x, which on simple comparisons alone can make the stock look expensive.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Rubrik is 7.69x. This proprietary estimate reflects what P/S might be expected given Rubrik’s earnings growth profile, its industry, profit margins, market cap and key risks. Because it pulls these inputs together in one number, the Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a basic check against peers or the overall industry.

Comparing the current 8.61x P/S to the 7.69x Fair Ratio suggests Rubrik screens as overvalued on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/S ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Rubrik Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. This is where Narratives come in, giving you a simple way to attach a story about Rubrik to the numbers you have seen, from fair value estimates through to future revenue, earnings and margins.

A Narrative on Simply Wall St is your own view on how a company’s business might develop, linked directly to a financial forecast and an implied fair value, rather than just a collection of disconnected metrics.

On the Community page, which is used by millions of investors, Narratives are set up as an easy tool so you can see how a particular story about Rubrik, such as stronger identity resilience adoption or slower AI agent take up, feeds into different assumptions and a different fair value.

Narratives then help you compare that fair value to Rubrik’s current share price to decide whether the gap between price and value looks attractive or stretched for your own approach, without telling you what to do.

Because Narratives update automatically when new information such as earnings or product news is added, your story and valuation stay aligned with the latest data rather than becoming stale.

For example, one Rubrik Narrative might lean closer to the higher analyst fair value of US$130.0, while another could sit nearer the lower US$65.27 view. Seeing both side by side helps you decide which story you find more realistic.

data.title: Eldorado Gold (TSX:ELD) Net Margin At 29.3% Tests Bearish Cost Pressure Narrative
data.description:
Eldorado Gold (TSX:ELD) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$532.4 million and basic EPS of US$0.69, setting a clear marker for its latest earnings season update. The company reported revenue of US$355.2 million in Q1 2025, US$435.7 million in Q4 2024, and US$532.4 million in Q1 2026, while basic EPS moved from US$0.36 to US$0.53 over that earlier period before landing at US$0.69 in the latest quarter. This puts the spotlight firmly on how sustainably its margins are being maintained.

See our full analysis for Eldorado Gold.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the strongest narratives around Eldorado Gold and where the recent margin picture supports or challenges those views.

See what the community is saying about Eldorado Gold

Advertisement

Net margin at 29.3% on US$2.0b revenue

  • Over the last 12 months, Eldorado Gold generated US$1.996b in revenue and US$584.3 million in net income, which works out to a 29.3% net margin compared with 23.9% a year earlier.
  • Consensus narrative points to commissioning of Skouries and site optimisations as key drivers for future margin strength, while the current 29.3% margin already reflects:
    • Higher profitability than the prior year margin of 23.9%, which lines up with the view that cost improvements at assets like Kişladağ and Lamaque are feeding through to the bottom line.
    • Trailing earnings growth of roughly 72% over the last year versus a 5 year average of 50.2% per year, which highlights how operational changes and higher realised gold prices are having a material effect on profitability.
Skouries moving toward first production and a 29.3% net margin already in place is the type of setup bullish investors often focus on, so it is worth seeing how that full upside case is framed in detail 🐂 Eldorado Gold Bull Case.

EPS and revenue growth test the bearish case

  • On a trailing basis, Basic EPS sits at US$2.90 and total revenue at US$1.996b, compared with US$1.48 EPS and US$1.323b revenue a year earlier, which is a step up even though Q1 2026 EPS of US$0.69 is lower than Q4 2025 EPS of US$1.26.
  • Bears focus on rising costs and project risk, yet the data show:
    • Trailing 12 month earnings growth of about 72% against a 5 year average of 50.2% per year, which contrasts with the view that higher input costs are already choking profit growth across the portfolio.
    • Forecast annual growth of about 20.7% for earnings and 20.5% for revenue, so the backward looking numbers currently sit in a range that bearish analysts consider hard to sustain if permitting issues in Greece and Turkey or elevated all in sustaining costs persist.
Skeptics often stress cost pressure and project risk, so comparing those concerns with the company’s recent growth record helps frame how cautious the bearish case really is 🐻 Eldorado Gold Bear Case.

Valuation gap versus 29.3% margin

  • With the share price at CA$40.53, Eldorado Gold is trading on a 13.3x P/E, below the Canadian Metals and Mining industry at 16.4x and peers at 18.5x, while a DCF fair value of CA$77.65 and an analyst target of CA$65.21 both sit above the current price.
  • What stands out for bullish investors is how the valuation compares with the fundamentals:
    • The combination of a 29.3% net margin and trailing revenue of just under US$2.0b means earnings are larger in absolute terms and are being valued at a discount to the peer group on P/E.
    • At the same time, the risk work flags a high proportion of non cash earnings and recent shareholder dilution, which gives context to why the P/E multiple might be lower even with a DCF fair value of CA$77.65 pointing to a higher intrinsic value estimate.

Next Steps

With both risks and rewards in play, the story around Eldorado Gold is finely balanced. Take a closer look at the details and weigh the 4 key rewards and 3 important warning signs.

data.title: MasTec (MTZ) Q1 Earnings Show 2.9% Net Margin That Tests Bullish Expansion Narrative
data.description:
MasTec (MTZ) opened Q1 2026 with revenue of about US$3.8b and basic EPS of US$0.78, setting the tone for how the rest of the year may shape up on both the top and bottom line. Over the past six reported quarters, revenue has moved from US$3.4b in Q4 2024 to just under US$4.0b in Q3 2025 before landing at US$3.8b in the latest quarter. Quarterly basic EPS has ranged from US$0.13 in Q1 2025 to US$2.07 in Q3 2025 and stands at US$0.78 in Q1 2026. With trailing twelve month net income of US$450.0m on revenue of about US$15.3b, the key question is how much of that volume is translating into sustainable margin quality.

See our full analysis for MasTec.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the widely shared narratives about MasTec's growth, risks and long term profitability story.

See what the community is saying about MasTec

Advertisement

TTM earnings growth runs ahead of revenue

  • Over the last twelve months, MasTec generated about US$15.3b of revenue and US$450.0m of net income, compared with US$14.3b and US$399.0m a year earlier. Earnings growth of 110.4% has outpaced the roughly US$1.0b step up in sales.
  • Bulls argue that stronger project execution and higher margin work across energy, communications, and power infrastructure can support this earnings momentum. However, the current 2.9% net margin and forecast 9.6% revenue growth leave less room for error than the more optimistic view suggests.
    • The bullish narrative looks for revenue growth of 12.5% to 16.6% a year and margin expansion toward 4.3% to 4.8%. This is higher than the 9.6% revenue growth forecast in the core data set.
    • If earnings are already growing faster than revenue on a 2.9% margin base, the key question for the bullish case is whether that margin lift actually shows up in future TTM numbers or whether growth moderates toward the 9.6% revenue outlook.
MasTec’s recent earnings run gives bulls plenty to point to. The gap between 9.6% revenue forecasts and the higher growth rates in the optimistic narrative is where the real debate starts, and that is exactly what bulls try to unpack in the 🐂 MasTec Bull Case.

Margins improve, but stay thin at 2.9%

  • Net profit margin for the last year sits at 2.9% versus 1.7% the prior year. Roughly US$449.979m of net income came from about US$15.28b of revenue, which is still a relatively low margin for an engineering and construction business.
  • Critics highlight that margin pressures from complex projects and ramp up costs could limit how far this improvement goes, even though analysts in the balanced narrative expect margins to move toward 4.3% over the next few years.
    • The bearish narrative points to project execution risk and the possible drag from large pipeline completions, which could make it harder to shift from a 2.9% net margin toward the 4.0% to 4.3% range that bearish and consensus views use in their 2029 earnings paths.
    • At the same time, the year on year move from 1.7% to 2.9% shows that some margin recovery is already in the trailing numbers. This challenges the idea that cost issues are uniformly limiting profitability across the portfolio.

Approach 1: Constellium Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value. For Constellium, this is done using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model that focuses on cash available to shareholders in US$.

Constellium’s last twelve month free cash flow is US$92.1 million. Analyst and model projections then extend this forward, including an estimate of US$417.3 million in free cash flow by 2028. Simply Wall St carries these forecasts out over 10 years, combining analyst inputs where available and extrapolations after that to build a full cash flow path.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$43.45 per share. Compared with the current share price of about US$32.01, the model suggests the stock is trading at a 26.3% discount. On these cash flow assumptions, this points to Constellium being undervalued.

Approach 2: Constellium Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to gauge what you are paying for each dollar of earnings, which is why it is a common anchor for valuations. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk usually justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to line up with a lower, more cautious multiple.

Constellium currently trades on a P/E of 15.96x. That sits below the Metals and Mining industry average P/E of 22.15x and below the peer group average of 48.63x.
